content:
4 min read Just now -- Press enter or click to view image in full size Most explanations of Hooks start with: “Hooks are functions that let you use state and other React features in functional components.” That definition is correct, but it doesn’t explain why Hooks exist, how React knows which state belongs to which hook, or what actually happens when a hook runs. Let’s build the understanding from the ground up. Before Hooks Existed Originally React had two kinds of components: // Functional Component function App() { return

Hello

; }These components were simple. They only received props and returned JSX. They could not • store state • use lifecycle methods • access refs • optimize rendering • use context If you needed any of these, you had to write: class App extends React.Component { state = { count: 0 }; componentDidMount(){} render(){ return

{this.state.count}

; } } React’s powerful features were tied to classes. Classes worked, but they introduced problems: • confusing this • constructor boilerplate • duplicated lifecycle logic • difficult code reuse • harder optimization React wanted all these capabilities inside plain JavaScript functions. That became possible with Hooks. So what actually is a Hook? A Hook is not a feature itself. It is simply a function that allows a normal JavaScript function (your component) to communicate with React’s internal engine. Think of it like this. Without hooks your component is just a function. function App() { return

Hello

; }React calls it. It returns JSX. Function finishes. Everything inside disappears. There is no memory. Now suppose you write function App() { const [count, setCount] = useState(0); return

{count}

; }Immediately one question should come to mind. Where is count stored? Certainly not inside the function. Because after execution the function is gone. So where does it live? The answer: Inside React. Hooks are a bridge When React executes useState(0)it does not simply create a variable. Instead it says “React, I need a piece of memory for this component.” React replies “Okay, I’ll store it for you.” So const [count, setCount] = useState(0);really means something like React, Please reserve one memory slot for me. Initial value = 0. Whenever I render again, give me this value back. The state belongs to React, not your function. Your component merely receives it. Why are they called Hooks? Because they “hook into” React’s internal system. Imagine React as a huge machine. Normally your component just enters and exits. Component↓ returns JSX ↓ Done Hooks allow the component to connect to React’s internal features. Component↓ useState() ↓ React State System ↓ useEffect() ↓ React Effect System ↓ useContext() ↓ React Context System ↓ returns JSX Each Hook connects your component to one part of React. That’s literally why they’re called Hooks. Hooks don’t execute independently This is a very common misunderstanding. People think Componentcalls ↓ Hook Actually it’s the opposite. React is controlling everything. React↓ calls component ↓ component calls hook ↓ hook communicates back to React ↓ React stores information ↓ React finishes rendering The hook is really a request to React. What happens internally? Suppose function App() { const [count, setCount] = useState(0); return

{count}

; }React starts rendering. Render begins↓ App() ↓ useState(0) ↓ React checks Does this component already have state? ↓ No ↓ Create one ↓ Store 0 ↓ Return [0, setCount] ↓ Component continues ↓ Returns JSX Later you click setCount(1);Notice something. setCount never changes count. Instead it tells React Please update this state.React stores 0 ↓ 1marks the component for an update, calls App()again, and this time useState(0)doesn’t create a new state. It simply returns 1because React already has it stored. Why can Hooks only be called at the top level? This rule exists because React identifies Hooks by the order in which they are called, not by their variable names. Imagine: function App(){ useState(); useEffect(); useRef(); }React internally remembers something like: Hook #1 → StateHook #2 → Effect Hook #3 → Ref On the next render, it expects the same sequence. If you write: if (loggedIn) { useState(); }useEffect(); One render might look like: #1 State #2 EffectAnother render might become: #1 EffectNow React would mistakenly think the effect is the state hook, shifting every subsequent hook. This is why Hooks must always be called in the same order on every render. Why do Hooks start with “use”? Because React needs to recognize them. Functions beginning with useare treated as Hooks. Example useState() useEffect() useMemo()Even your own custom Hook follows the same convention: function useFetchData() {}React’s lint rules use this naming convention to enforce the Rules of Hooks, making it easier to catch mistakes before your code runs. The deeper idea A React component is not an object. It has no fields. No properties. No permanent memory. Every render is just another function call. Hooks solve that limitation by letting React attach persistent data and behavior to an otherwise stateless function. So the component stays simple and declarative, while React keeps track of everything that must survive across renders — state, effects, refs, memoized values, reducers, and more. One sentence definition A Hook is a special function that lets a functional component connect to React’s internal systems (such as state, effects, context, refs, and scheduling), while React stores and manages the underlying data across renders. Once you understand Hooks as a communication layer between your component and React’s internal engine, every individual Hook — useState, useEffect, useRef, useMemo, and the rest—becomes much easier to reason about.
